utterances: Interesting.
Bill Nelson won that seat.
We're talking with Political Junkie Ken Rudin.
Up next, the blame game on the budget.
If the government shuts down on Friday night, who will get the blame?
Our - political columnist Michael Gerson will join us from the Washington Post.
Give us a call: 800-989-8255.
Email us: talk@npr.org.
Stay with us.
I'm Neal Conan.
It's the TALK OF THE NATION, from NPR News.
(Soundbite of music)
This is TALK OF THE NATION, from NPR News.
I'm Neal Conan.
The Political Junkie is back with us.
Ken Rudin wouldn't miss a budget showdown.
NPR's political editor, you can read Ken's column, listen to his podcast and do your best to solve his ScuttleButton puzzle all at npr.org/junkie.
If Congress can't agree on a 2011 budget or another stopgap measure, people will wake up Saturday with parts of the federal government shut down.
Leaders from the House and Senate tried and failed to reach a deal yesterday at the White House.
The president took to the podium afterwards and complained that Republicans would not meet him halfway.

We can't have a my-way-or-the-highway approach to this problem because if we start applying that approach, where I've got to get 110 percent of everything I want or else I'm going to shut down the government, we're not going to get anything done this year, and the American people are going to be the ones that suffer.
The GOP, led by Speaker of the House John Boehner, pushed right back.

And we're not going to allow the Senate nor the White House to put us in a box where we have to make a choice between two bad options of cutting a bad deal this week in order to keep the government open or allow the government to shut down.
Both parties point the finger at each other, as the clock continues to tick down to midnight Friday.
If there's a government shutdown, who will get the blame?
Email talk@npr.org.
Joining us here in Studio 3A is Michael Gerson, a former speechwriter for President George W.
Bush, now a columnist for the Washington Post, where his column, ""Cornered by Obama,"" ran yesterday.
And Michael Gerson, nice to have you back with us.
Great to be with you.
And John Boehner talked about not being put in a box.
You wrote that is precisely where he is.
I think he's precisely where he doesn't want to be.
You know, I think the president has a pretty weak fiscal record, but he's in a strong negotiating position right now.
He accepted a $33 billion top line for reductions, which is pretty much what Republicans started out with as a negotiating position last February.

And now, when they reject that, he's the one that seems like the moderate voice, the reasonable voice in this debate, and it pretty much assures that if there were a shutdown, which I still don't think is going to happen, that Republicans would get the lion's share of the blame.
Well, we're going to talk more about what's going on today, but tell us about this tactical box that John Boehner is in.
You wrote that Barack Obama has achieved, from a very weak hand, you say, he's achieved the not insignificant task of splitting the Republican Party.
No, I agree with that.
And the reality here is the Republican Party is split.
That's why the president can make this kind of case.
You have people like Paul Ryan that are very much focused on the 2012 budget: ambitious structural reform, you know, Medicare reform, tax reform, a lot of other things.

Then you have other Republicans, Tea Party freshmen and people like Mike Pence and others, who are very much focused on the 2011 budget and squeezing a few billion dollars extra out of this current situation.
And that's - I think that there's a difference in both stature, strategy and sophistication between those two approaches.
I think - you know, Ryan is much more ambitious, and I think Boehner needs to convince his own conference that that's the fight they want to focus on, not this one, the shutdown fight.
You mentioned the Tea Party.
Amy Kremer, the chair of the Tea Party Express, told the Washington crowd she's not okay with any compromise on the budget.
I don't think it's a good thing to shut down our government, but I do think that they need to do what they said they were going to do.
They said they were going to cut $100 billion, and they had time.
They knew that they had won the House.
They had time to work on this, to figure out how to do it.
And you had not just the Tea Party spokespeople like that but Mike Pence and Michele Bachmann chanting: Shut it down, shut it down.
Yes, I mean, both of those leaders are people that want to position themselves as rebel leaders to lead this kind of movement.
But this is the problem.
I mean, the House of Representatives is one-half of one branch of government.
It would normally, under normal circumstances, be seen as a very good deal to get, you know, $33 billion in cuts under these circumstances.
They don't control both houses.
And I think a lot of the committee chairmen, from what I'm told, view it as a pretty good deal.
But there's a significant portion of that base that has much higher expectations, which may be, in this circumstance, unrealistic expectations.

Michael, in your column yesterday, which was absolutely excellent, you talk about how you see the very astute and great political skills of President Obama and the White House team, led by chief of staff William Daley.
But conversely, isn't it the lack of political skills by the House Republicans and the lessons that they didn't learn from '95, '96 that we're seeing all over again?
Well, I think if they went towards a shutdown and accepted that, rather than split the caucus, that that would be a tremendous mistake.
In this circumstance, you would literally cut off, in the middle of a pay period, the people in Afghanistan.
You know, that would be a tremendous signal.
Now, that would probably be made up later, but it would be a terrible signal from the Republican Party.
And tax day coming up right around the corner, too.
Exactly.
You know, I think Democrats could also make the case that this is bad for the broader economy.
It's going to cost money to have a shutdown.
Right, exactly.
And also, you know, just as we're headed into a little bit of a recovery, to have that kind of measure can't be good for the economy.
And so I think there are going to be a bunch of arguments.
Now, as I mentioned earlier, I'm not sure we're going to get there.
I do believe that Boehner really wants to avoid a shutdown.
He's already filed a bill in the House for another one-week continuing resolution.
The president said he wouldn't sign it.
I know, but he could bring that up on Friday, and that would be a very difficult thing to turn down if you're - you know, under the right circumstances.
That would just postpone briefly the problem.
But I think that Boehner, at least, does not view a shutdown as a good strategy.

The speaker, though, could take that $33 billion, and there are some other issues that are involved, but he could take that to the floor and win with probably, what, 50, 60 Democratic votes and get it passed.
It would cost him, though, the support of, well, what some have called the perfectionist wing of the Republican Party.
Yes, and they're already looking for excuses for discontent.
And maybe there's not much you can do about that, but I think that Boehner does need to look like he's pushing for the last little bit before he makes a compromise.
And, you know, he could maybe get up to $40 billion in negotiations, and he could take that to the Tea Party wing and say: I made progress.
Let's get some callers in on the conversation.
If there is a shutdown, who's going to get the blame?
We'll start with Mary(ph), Mary with us from Leavenworth in Kansas.
Yes, and I think it'll be the Democratic Party that had been in - they had control of Congress for the last couple of years, and they haven't even produced a budget of any sorts within that time.
So it will be the Democrats that will pay, and Mr. Obama had all this time to work out something instead of taking vacation over here and there.
He ought to be here, standing here to run this country and run it right instead of trying to take care of everybody else's country.
So it'll be the Democrats.
Mary's got a point, Michael Gerson.
She does.
This is the 2011 budget.
It was supposed to be passed this time last year.
Right.
I think there's a valid point there.
If - when the Democratic Party controlled both houses, if they had passed a 2011 budget, we wouldn't be in this circumstance.
You had both houses and the president in that position.
So I think it's a valid critique.
But I'm not - right now I think the political circumstance is quite different because the president does have an offer on the table to make this work.
Let's go next to Anna(ph), Anna with us from Nashville.
